psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q1.1: A sunk cost is by definition irrecoverable; money or effort already spent cannot be gotten back.

psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q2.0: Past spending is irrelevant to future value; focusing on it is the sunk cost fallacy.
psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q2.1: Time already invested is also a sunk cost and should not drive future decisions.
psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q2.3: What other people think is social pressure, not rational economic analysis.

psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q3.0: Going because you already spent $100 is the sunk cost fallacy in action.
psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q3.2: The money is already gone whether you go or not; attending sick just adds misery.
psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q3.3: Selling is an option but is not presented as the best rational choice in this scenario.

psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q5.0: The Concorde was funded despite rational analysis showing it would never be profitable.
psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q5.2: The Concorde was actually one of the most expensive aircraft programs in history.
psy-sec6-u6-L1-Q5.3: The project continued far too long precisely because of sunk cost thinking, not early cancellation.

psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q1.1: Each additional investment raises the psychological stakes, making it progressively harder to walk away.

psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q2.0: Escalation of commitment predicts the opposite: doubling down rather than cutting losses.
psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q2.2: Asking customers is a rational step, but the bias predicts skipping rational evaluation.
psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q2.3: Rationally evaluating future potential is what people should do, but the bias prevents it.

psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q4.0: Asking how much you have spent keeps you focused on sunk costs rather than future value.
psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q4.2: What others think introduces social pressure, not rational analysis.
psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q4.3: Asking how much more to spend still frames the decision around the sunk cost.

psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q5.1: Staying to avoid wasting 3 years is the sunk cost fallacy; those years are gone regardless.
psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q5.2: Taking a break without changing course does not address the fundamental dissatisfaction.
psy-sec6-u6-L2-Q5.3: Delegating a major life decision to others avoids personal responsibility and sunk cost analysis.

psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q1.1: Status quo bias is exactly this preference for the current state, even when better alternatives exist.

psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q2.0: Switching banks to get a better rate shows openness to change, not status quo bias.
psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q2.2: Comparing all options is rational analysis, not a bias.
psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q2.3: Trying new restaurants every week shows novelty-seeking, the opposite of status quo bias.

psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q5.1: Anchoring bias involves initial numbers setting a reference point, not resistance to changing software.
psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q5.2: Confirmation bias would involve seeking positive reviews of the old software, not simply refusing to switch.
psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q5.3: The availability heuristic involves overestimating frequency of recalled events, not software preferences.

psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q6.0: Change is not always risky; sometimes staying the same carries greater risk.
psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q6.1: The current option is often not the best; the bias just makes it feel that way.
psy-sec6-u6-L3-Q6.3: Status quo bias shows people resist novelty; they do not naturally seek out the newest options.

psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q1.1: Research consistently shows opt-out defaults raise organ donation rates from about 15% to about 85%.

psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q2.0: Defaults are not always the best option; they are simply the pre-selected one.
psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q2.2: Most people accept defaults without careful evaluation, which is the whole point.
psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q2.3: No law requires accepting defaults; people simply tend not to change them.

psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q5.0: Emails are easily ignored and have low effectiveness compared to changing the default.
psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q5.2: Bonuses help but are far less effective than switching to an opt-out default.
psy-sec6-u6-L4-Q5.3: Requiring meetings adds friction, which would likely reduce participation rather than increase it.

psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q1.0: In-group bias appears even with randomly assigned, trivial groups, not just meaningful ones.

psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q2.0: The minimal group experiments found the opposite: people did not treat all equally.
psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q2.2: Participants did not favor the other group; they favored their own.
psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q2.3: Group assignment had a strong effect, even when the assignment was completely random.

psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q5.1: Anchoring bias involves numbers influencing estimates, not school affiliation influencing hiring.
psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q5.2: The availability heuristic involves ease of recall, not shared group membership.
psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q5.3: The framing effect involves how information is presented, not group-based favoritism.

psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q6.0: Hiring only from your alma mater is the definition of in-group bias, not a solution.
psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q6.2: Asking about group membership could increase rather than decrease bias.
psy-sec6-u7-L1-Q6.3: Gut feeling is where biases like in-group favoritism thrive.

psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q1.0: The halo effect creates a perception of intelligence; attractiveness does not actually predict smarter performance.

psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q2.0: Appearance and manners do not accurately measure intelligence.
psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q2.2: Anchoring bias involves initial numbers, not appearance influencing grades.
psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q2.3: This is about appearance creating a positive impression, not seeking confirming evidence about reputation.

psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q4.1: The halo effect creates positive impressions; being late would create a negative one.
psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q4.2: Anchoring involves numbers, not a negative impression from tardiness.
psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q4.3: Status quo bias involves preferring the current state, not negatively judging a late candidate.

psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q5.0: Celebrities rarely have genuine expertise in every product they endorse.
psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q5.2: Research shows celebrity endorsements significantly influence consumer decisions.
psy-sec6-u7-L2-Q5.3: The halo effect from celebrity endorsements works across all product categories, not just beauty.

psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q1.0: The fundamental attribution error is the opposite: we overemphasize personal character, not situational factors, when judging others.

psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q2.0: Considering their bad day would be giving situational context, which the attribution error makes us skip.
psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q2.2: Wondering what happened to them shows empathy that the attribution error typically overrides.
psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q2.3: Blaming the workplace is a situational explanation, which we tend not to give others.

psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q5.1: The attribution error predicts unequal treatment, not equal consideration.
psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q5.2: We typically blame the situation for our own behavior but character for others.
psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q5.3: We rarely blame our own character; we are much more generous with self-explanations.

psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q6.0: Assuming everyone has bad character is overly pessimistic and not a solution.
psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q6.2: Never judging anyone is impractical; the goal is fairer, more accurate judgments.
psy-sec6-u7-L3-Q6.3: Blaming situations for everything, including crimes, goes too far in the other direction.

psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q1.1: Stereotypes are indeed a type of heuristic: group-level generalizations used as mental shortcuts.

psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q2.0: The brain uses stereotypes automatically, not as a deliberate attempt to be unfair.
psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q2.2: Stereotypes are generalizations that are often inaccurate for specific individuals.
psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q2.3: Individuals can be evaluated; the brain just defaults to group shortcuts to save effort.

psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q3.0: Implicit biases affect everyone, including people with egalitarian beliefs.
psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q3.2: Implicit biases are difficult to detect precisely because they operate unconsciously.
psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q3.3: Implicit biases influence snap judgments in everyday interactions, not just major decisions.

psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q6.1: Both groups had equal math ability; the difference was caused by stereotype activation.
psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q6.2: The instructions contained a stereotype, not math hints.
psy-sec6-u7-L4-Q6.3: The consistent group-level effect rules out random chance as an explanation.

psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q1.1: Decision fatigue is well-documented: each successive decision depletes mental resources, leading to poorer choices.

psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q2.0: Offense severity was not correlated with time of day in the study.
psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q2.2: The judges were not biased against afternoon applicants; they were mentally depleted.
psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q2.3: Case scheduling was not based on difficulty; fatigue drove the pattern.

psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q4.0: Jobs was a billionaire; affordability was not the reason.
psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q4.2: Jobs cared about design and aesthetics deeply; the uniform was a deliberate strategy.
psy-sec6-u8-L1-Q4.3: Apple had no such dress code; this was a personal decision to conserve mental energy.

psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q1.0: The jam study showed the opposite: offering 24 options led to only 3% buying, versus 30% with 6 options.

psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q2.0: People do want some choice, just not an overwhelming number of options.
psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q2.2: The jam study showed that more options led to worse decisions and less satisfaction.
psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q2.3: Most people find extensive comparison stressful, not enjoyable.

psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q3.1: Adding more items would worsen the choice paralysis that is already causing problems.
psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q3.2: Removing descriptions would make choosing harder, not easier.
psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q3.3: Pressuring customers to choose quickly creates stress without solving the underlying overload.

psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q6.0: The jam study proved the opposite: too many options decreased both purchases and satisfaction.
psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q6.2: Jam was the study material, not a finding about grocery popularity.
psy-sec6-u8-L2-Q6.3: The study showed that the number of options, not price, was the key variable.

psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q1.0: Satisficers are generally happier because they do not agonize over whether a better option exists.

psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q3.0: Maximizers often find objectively better options; the issue is satisfaction, not quality.
psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q3.2: Satisficers and maximizers have similar taste; the difference is search strategy.
psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q3.3: Maximizers care deeply about quality, which is precisely what drives their exhausting search.

psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q6.1: Comparing 47 more models is maximizing behavior that has already caused 3 weeks of paralysis.
psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q6.2: Waiting indefinitely is avoidance, not a decision strategy.
psy-sec6-u8-L3-Q6.3: Delegating to 20 friends adds more opinions and complexity rather than resolving the paralysis.

psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q1.0: Biases run on automatic System 1 processes that knowledge alone cannot override.

psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q2.0: People can understand biases intellectually but still fall victim to them automatically.
psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q2.2: Biases affect everyone, including those who have studied them extensively.
psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q2.3: Knowledge does not make biases stronger; it just fails to eliminate automatic processes.

psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q4.0: Trying harder relies on willpower, which is unreliable against automatic biases.
psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q4.2: Avoiding all decisions is impractical and does not count as debiasing.
psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q4.3: The day of the week has no special relevance to debiasing effectiveness.

psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q6.1: Knowledge without structured processes is insufficient to prevent automatic biases.
psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q6.2: Structured processes are significantly more effective than relying on self-awareness alone.
psy-sec6-u9-L1-Q6.3: Research shows structured debiasing tools make measurable improvements in decision quality.

psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q1.1: This technique works precisely by forcing you to seek and consider evidence that contradicts your view.

psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q2.0: Anchoring bias involves initial numbers, not seeking confirming evidence.
psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q2.2: The endowment effect involves overvaluing owned items, not selective evidence seeking.
psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q2.3: Decision fatigue is about depleted mental energy, not biased evidence seeking.

psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q5.1: Unanimous enthusiasm without criticism is a classic sign of groupthink, not a green light.
psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q5.2: Voting confirms agreement but does not challenge the idea or surface hidden weaknesses.
psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q5.3: Canceling the project is an overreaction; the goal is to stress-test the idea, not kill it.

psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q6.0: The goal is not to prove you were right; it is to track how your reasoning evolves.
psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q6.2: Saving time is a minor benefit; the main purpose is preventing retroactive reasoning shifts.
psy-sec6-u9-L2-Q6.3: Impressing others is not the purpose; creating personal accountability for your reasoning is.

psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q1.0: A pre-mortem is conducted before the project starts, imagining future failure to identify risks now.

psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q2.1: Anxiety is not the mechanism; the technique works by shifting the cognitive framing of failure.
psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q2.2: No technique eliminates all risk; pre-mortems help identify and mitigate risks.
psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q2.3: The technique works because of cognitive reframing, not because failure is enjoyable.

psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q4.0: Pre-mortems address much more than just anchoring bias.
psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q4.1: Pre-mortems go far beyond the availability heuristic to tackle multiple planning biases.
psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q4.3: Sunk cost fallacy is about past investment, not future risk assessment.

psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q5.1: Increasing budget does not address the hidden risks that overconfidence is masking.
psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q5.2: Launching immediately before examining risks is exactly the overconfidence trap.
psy-sec6-u9-L3-Q5.3: External consultants cannot guarantee success; the team needs to confront its own blind spots.

psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q1.1: Research demonstrates that surgical checklists reduce complications by approximately 40%.

psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q2.0: Checklists may slightly slow things down but their main benefit is reducing errors.
psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q2.2: Checklists reduce but do not permanently eliminate all biases.
psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q2.3: Checklists are effective in many fields beyond surgery, including aviation and business.

psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q3.1: Trusting gut feelings is exactly what allows biases to infiltrate hiring decisions.
psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q3.2: Hiring only from your alma mater is in-group bias, not a debiasing strategy.
psy-sec6-u9-L4-Q3.3: Random hiring would be unfair and ineffective; structured evaluation is the answer.

psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q3.0: A post-mortem is conducted after a project ends, not before.
psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q3.2: A devil\u2019s advocate argues against proposals but does not specifically imagine failure scenarios.
psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q3.3: A checklist ensures steps are followed but does not involve imagining project failure.

psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q5.1: Anchoring alone does not explain the combination of news fear, 10-year habit, and risk aversion.
psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q5.2: The framing effect alone does not explain the three distinct bias patterns present.
psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q5.3: Multiple biases are clearly operating in this scenario.

psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q6.0: Complete elimination of biases is impossible because they are automatic processes.
psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q6.2: Perfect rationality is unachievable; biases are a fundamental part of human cognition.
psy-sec6-u10-L1-Q6.3: Studying biases is about self-improvement, not proving others are flawed.

psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q1.1: Anchoring bias involves initial numbers influencing estimates, not protecting past investments.
psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q1.2: The availability heuristic involves ease of recall, not reluctance to abandon spending.
psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q1.3: The halo effect involves positive trait spillover, not investment decisions.

psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q2.1: Three diagnoses do not indicate greater talent than 20 years of seasoned experience.
psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q2.2: The supervisor's humility reflects expert calibration, not imposter syndrome.
psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q2.3: The nurse and supervisor show clearly different bias patterns, not equal ones.

psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q3.1: Anchoring bias involves numbers setting reference points, not streak-based fear messaging.
psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q3.2: In-group bias involves favoring your own group members, not app renewal decisions.
psy-sec6-u10-L2-Q3.3: The halo effect involves positive trait spillover, not loss-framed subscription tactics.
